turns.confidence_note: Turn 1 response — cannot ground in party-specific perception because no party behavior has been observed yet. Response appropriately focuses on process framing and invitation rather than content.
turns.message_text:
Thank you both for being here. I'm Solomon, and I'll be supporting this mediation session.

Before we begin, I want to frame what this process is and what it isn't. This is a structured space for both of you to work through the issues you're facing in your separation. My role is to help you surface what matters to each of you, identify where your interests might align, and explore options that could work for both of you. I don't take sides, I don't make decisions for you, and I'm not here to provide legal advice.

A few ground rules: Each of you will have uninterrupted time to speak. I'll ask questions to understand your concerns more deeply. If at any point either of you feels unsafe or needs to pause, you can say so. This process works best when both of you participate freely.

I'd like to start by hearing from each of you about what brings you here today and what concerns matter most to you right now. Who would like to begin?

response_synthesis.message_summary: Session opening — establish process framework, set ground rules, and invite parties to describe their concerns
response_synthesis.message_text:
Thank you both for being here. I'm Solomon, and I'll be supporting this mediation session.

Before we begin, I want to frame what this process is and what it isn't. This is a structured space for both of you to work through the issues you're facing in your separation. My role is to help you surface what matters to each of you, identify where your interests might align, and explore options that could work for both of you. I don't take sides, I don't make decisions for you, and I'm not here to provide legal advice.

A few ground rules: Each of you will have uninterrupted time to speak. I'll ask questions to understand your concerns more deeply. If at any point either of you feels unsafe or needs to pause, you can say so. This process works best when both of you participate freely.

I'd like to start by hearing from each of you about what brings you here today and what concerns matter most to you right now. Who would like to begin?

Option assessment requires at minimum that both parties have had opportunity to describe their concerns and that the issue type has been identified.

This blocking assessment will naturally resolve once both parties have had opportunity to speak and the issue domain becomes clear.

turns.message_text:
Thank you for that, Party A. I want to make sure I'm holding your concern clearly — you're saying that when holidays or school breaks involve schedule changes, having a clear notice window is important so your child isn't caught off guard by last-minute shifts. I've noted that.

Before we go further, I want to bring Party B into the conversation. Party B, I'd like to hear from you. You can respond to what Party A just raised — whether notice windows for schedule changes is something you share concern about, or whether you see it differently. Or, if there's a different issue that's more pressing for you right now, you're welcome to put that on the table. What's most important for you as we start this process?

domain_analysis.domain_notes: The domain reasoner correctly identifies this as blocked — not because the issue is unclear (Party A's concern is specific and understandable) but because mediation process requires bilateral input before option work can responsibly begin. Party A has named a procedural need; we cannot assess whether that need conflicts with Party B's constraints, whether Party B shares the concern, or whether Party B has different priorities until Party B speaks. The issue structure (notice protocols for schedule changes) is a well-formed mediation problem, but we are at the beginning of information gathering.

candidate_options: Tiered notice requirement by schedule type
Default schedule with opt-in variation protocol
Child transition preparation protocol tied to notice periods
Graduated notice windows by calendar proximity
Shared digital calendar with automatic notice triggers
option_scan.deferral_reason: Party B has not been heard. All candidate options require understanding both parties' constraints and interests. Generating options now would risk: (1) presenting solutions that don't account for Party B's needs, (2) signaling to Party B that their input is less important than Party A's, (3) foreclosing option space before the full problem is understood. The correct move is to invite Party B to respond to Party A's concern and/or introduce their own priorities.

response_synthesis.message_text:
Thank you for that, Party A. I want to make sure I'm holding your concern clearly — you're saying that when holidays or school breaks involve schedule changes, having a clear notice window is important so your child isn't caught off guard by last-minute shifts. I've noted that.

Before we go further, I want to bring Party B into the conversation. Party B, I'd like to hear from you. You can respond to what Party A just raised — whether notice windows for schedule changes is something you share concern about, or whether you see it differently. Or, if there's a different issue that's more pressing for you right now, you're welcome to put that on the table. What's most important for you as we start this process?
response_synthesis.confidence_note: Low perception confidence is appropriate — we have limited signal from Party A and no signal from Party B. The response is deliberately open-ended to allow Party B to shape the agenda rather than assuming Party A's concern is the only or primary issue.

pre_computed_domain_analysis.readiness_rationale: Only Party A has been heard (T2). Party B has not yet responded or provided any input on their interests, constraints, or reaction to Party A's notice-window concern. This is a fundamental information absence, not a logistical gap. Domain qualification requires understanding both parties' positions to assess feasibility of coordination mechanisms. The issue structure (schedule change protocols) is clear, but proceeding without Party B's perspective would be premature and potentially harmful to the mediation process.

blocking_constraints.constraint: Party B has not been heard
blocking_constraints.what_would_resolve_it: Party B needs opportunity to respond to Party A's notice concern and articulate their own interests regarding schedule flexibility, spontaneity, or practical constraints that affect their ability to provide advance notice.

material_gaps.gap: Current custody schedule structure (alternating weeks, 2-2-3, weekday/weekend split, etc.)
material_gaps.what_it_blocks: Cannot assess feasibility of tiered notice systems or default schedule options without knowing the baseline structure that changes would affect.

material_gaps.gap: Geographic proximity of parents and work/life constraints
material_gaps.what_it_blocks: Cannot assess practicality of last-minute flexibility, digital calendar coordination, or annual planning sessions without knowing logistical realities.
Cannot calibrate notice windows appropriate to child's needs (age-gated options) or assess transition preparation protocols without developmental context.

material_gaps.gap: Historical pattern of schedule changes — frequency, type, conflict points
material_gaps.what_it_blocks: Cannot determine whether this is a sporadic issue requiring light structure or chronic conflict requiring robust protocols. Cannot assess which change categories cause most friction.

pre_computed_domain_analysis.domain_notes: This is turn 3 in an info-gathering phase. Party A has articulated a clear process concern (notice windows for holiday/school-break changes), but we are still at the beginning of bilateral information gathering. Party B's absence from the record is not a 'gap' to be filled with assumptions — it is the current state of a mediation that has only just begun hearing from parties. The brainstormer has generated structurally sound options for schedule-change protocols, but domain qualification requires knowing whether Party B shares the predictability concern, has competing flexibility needs, faces practical constraints on advance planning, or disputes that last-minute changes are occurring. Without Party B's input, I cannot assess feasibility of coordination mechanisms that bind both parties. The correct posture is to defer option work until Party B has been heard, at which point many of these candidates will likely become qualifiable at moderate confidence.

domain_expert_candidates.label: Right of first refusal with notice standard
domain_expert_candidates.rationale: Standard family law mechanism when schedule changes involve alternative care. If one parent cannot use their scheduled time, other parent gets first option before third-party care is arranged. Addresses both predictability (through notice standard) and flexibility (through substitution mechanism). Commonly used in custody agreements.

party_interest_alignment.party_a: Provides advance notice and reduces last-minute surprises; gives Party A additional time with child when Party B's plans change
party_interest_alignment.party_b: Unknown — depends on whether Party B values flexibility to arrange alternative care independently or would accept reciprocal first-refusal arrangement

domain_expert_candidates.label: Make-up time standard for missed parenting time
domain_expert_candidates.rationale: When schedule changes result in one parent losing time, this establishes whether and how that time is made up. Can be paired with notice requirements (adequate notice = make-up time required; inadequate notice = no make-up). Addresses fairness dimension of schedule changes.

party_interest_alignment.party_a: If Party A's concern includes equity (not just predictability), make-up time provision may be important
party_interest_alignment.party_b: Unknown — depends on whether Party B views schedule as strict entitlements or flexible coordination

domain_blocked.label: Tiered notice requirement by schedule type
domain_blocked.blocking_rationale: Party B has not been heard. Cannot assess whether Party B can comply with tiered notice windows, whether they have legitimate need for shorter notice in certain contexts, or whether they dispute that last-minute changes are occurring. Structural feasibility depends on both parties' practical constraints.
domain_blocked.what_would_unblock: Party B input on their scheduling constraints, flexibility needs, and reaction to Party A's concern.

domain_blocked.label: Default schedule with opt-in variation protocol
domain_blocked.blocking_rationale: Party B has not been heard. Cannot assess whether Party B would accept a default schedule baseline, whether they need variation pathways for legitimate reasons, or what their underlying interests are regarding schedule stability vs. flexibility.
domain_blocked.what_would_unblock: Party B input on their interests in schedule flexibility and their constraints on advance planning.

domain_blocked.label: Child transition preparation protocol tied to notice periods
domain_blocked.blocking_rationale: Party B has not been heard. Cannot assess whether Party B shares the child-centered framing, whether they have different views on appropriate transition preparation, or whether they can comply with notice triggers given their practical constraints.
domain_blocked.what_would_unblock: Party B input on their approach to child transitions and their ability to provide advance notice.

domain_blocked.label: Emergency override with post-hoc review mechanism
domain_blocked.blocking_rationale: Party B has not been heard. Cannot assess what constitutes 'emergency' from both parties' perspectives, whether post-hoc review would be workable for them, or whether Party B's schedule-change pattern includes legitimate emergency/opportunity scenarios.
domain_blocked.what_would_unblock: Party B input on types of schedule changes they need to make and circumstances that prevent advance notice.

domain_blocked.label: Graduated notice windows by calendar proximity
domain_blocked.blocking_rationale: Party B has not been heard. Cannot assess whether Party B can comply with graduated windows, whether their planning horizon matches the proposed structure, or whether they have constraints that require different notice calibration.
domain_blocked.what_would_unblock: Party B input on their planning constraints and typical schedule-change scenarios.

domain_blocked.label: Shared digital calendar with automatic notice triggers
domain_blocked.blocking_rationale: Party B has not been heard. Cannot assess technological capacity, willingness to use shared digital tools, or whether this approach would address Party B's interests and constraints. Also cannot assess current communication pattern or whether digital coordination would improve or complicate it.
domain_blocked.what_would_unblock: Party B input on communication preferences and technological comfort; understanding of current coordination mechanisms.

domain_blocked.label: Annual schedule-setting session with mid-year check-in
domain_blocked.blocking_rationale: Party B has not been heard. Cannot assess whether Party B can plan holidays/breaks annually in advance, whether they have work/life constraints that prevent this, or whether they would participate in joint planning sessions. Also cannot assess whether the relational dynamic supports collaborative planning.
domain_blocked.what_would_unblock: Party B input on their planning constraints and willingness to engage in joint scheduling sessions; understanding of co-parenting relationship quality.

domain_blocked.label: Notice requirement with automatic approval for Category A changes
domain_blocked.blocking_rationale: Party B has not been heard. Cannot categorize changes or determine automatic-approval categories without understanding what types of changes Party B typically needs to make and which categories cause conflict between parties.
domain_blocked.what_would_unblock: Both parties' input on which schedule changes are routine/non-controversial vs. which require mutual agreement; historical pattern of change types.

domain_blocked.label: First-refusal protocol for unexpected parent schedule changes
domain_blocked.blocking_rationale: Party B has not been heard. Cannot assess whether Party B's schedule changes involve alternative care arrangements, whether they would accept first-refusal constraints, or whether this addresses the core concern. Also overlaps with opt-dom-001 (domain expert candidate covering same mechanism).
domain_blocked.what_would_unblock: Party B input on their schedule-change scenarios and approach to alternative care arrangements.

domain_blocked.label: Child age-gated notice requirements with sunset review
domain_blocked.blocking_rationale: Party B has not been heard. Also missing child's current age and developmental stage. Cannot calibrate age-appropriate notice windows or assess whether developmental framing resonates with Party B's perspective on the issue.
domain_blocked.what_would_unblock: Party B input on their perspective; child's age and developmental needs; both parties' views on how notice requirements should evolve as child ages.

domain_blocked.label: Right of first refusal with notice standard
domain_blocked.blocking_rationale: Party B has not been heard. Cannot assess whether Party B's schedule changes involve alternative care scenarios where first-refusal would apply, or whether they would accept reciprocal first-refusal constraints.
domain_blocked.what_would_unblock: Party B input on their schedule-change patterns and approach to alternative care.

domain_blocked.label: Make-up time standard for missed parenting time
domain_blocked.blocking_rationale: Party B has not been heard. Cannot assess whether schedule changes result in lost time for either party, whether Party B views schedule as strict entitlements requiring make-up, or whether equity dimension is relevant to the conflict.
domain_blocked.what_would_unblock: Party B input on their interests in schedule flexibility vs. protected parenting time; understanding of whether changes are typically one-directional or reciprocal.

brainstormer_pool.label: Tiered notice requirement by schedule type
brainstormer_pool.rationale: Creates predictability while allowing flexibility for different contexts. Major changes (holidays/school breaks) require longer notice than minor adjustments. Addresses Party A's concern about child stability while leaving room for reasonable accommodation of unforeseen circumstances.

party_interest_alignment.party_a: Provides the clear boundaries and advance notice needed for planning and child preparation. Different tiers allow them to know what to expect based on change type.
party_interest_alignment.party_b: Preserves some flexibility for genuine emergencies or opportunities while establishing clear expectations. Not yet articulated but may value not being locked into rigid structure for all scenarios.

brainstormer_pool.label: Default schedule with opt-in variation protocol
brainstormer_pool.rationale: Establishes baseline predictability (Party A's core concern) while creating a structured pathway for requesting changes. Party requesting change bears burden of advance notice and documentation. Separates routine from exception.

party_interest_alignment.party_a: Default schedule provides the predictability and routine they've emphasized. Any departure requires following their notice-window concern.
party_interest_alignment.party_b: Maintains ability to request adjustments through clear process rather than being completely locked in. Process clarity may feel fairer than ambiguous negotiation.

brainstormer_pool.label: Child transition preparation protocol tied to notice periods
brainstormer_pool.rationale: Reframes notice requirement as child-centered practice rather than parent conflict. Notice window triggers specific preparation steps (telling child, coordinating belongings, etc.). Makes the 'why' of notice tangible and shared.

party_interest_alignment.party_a: Directly addresses their stated concern about child not being surprised. Makes their procedural request feel protective rather than controlling.
party_interest_alignment.party_b: If they value being seen as child-focused, this frames compliance as parenting practice rather than capitulation. May reduce defensiveness around Party A's request.

brainstormer_pool.label: Emergency override with post-hoc review mechanism
brainstormer_pool.rationale: Acknowledges that rigid notice requirements may conflict with genuine emergencies or opportunities (sick relative, unexpected work travel, special event). Creates safety valve while maintaining accountability through after-the-fact review.

party_interest_alignment.party_a: Preserves notice requirement as default while accepting that life happens. Review mechanism prevents emergency category from being abused.
party_interest_alignment.party_b: Provides flexibility for genuine unforeseen circumstances without having to fight for each exception. Knows what qualifies as override-worthy.

brainstormer_pool.label: Graduated notice windows by calendar proximity
brainstormer_pool.rationale: Notice requirement scales with how far in advance the event is. Holiday six months away requires less notice than holiday next week. Balances planning need against over-constraining long-term scheduling.

party_interest_alignment.party_a: Gets shorter notice windows for near-term changes where child disruption is more acute. Still provides advance planning capability.
party_interest_alignment.party_b: Avoids having to commit to distant-future plans too early. Can adjust long-range plans more easily than immediate ones.

brainstormer_pool.label: Shared digital calendar with automatic notice triggers
brainstormer_pool.rationale: Technological solution to procedural concern. Changes proposed in shared system automatically calculate notice compliance. Removes ambiguity and potential for after-the-fact dispute about whether notice was adequate.

party_interest_alignment.party_a: Provides clear documentation and removes possibility of last-minute surprise. System enforces the boundary they're seeking.
party_interest_alignment.party_b: Clarifies exactly what constitutes adequate notice. No room for miscommunication or good-faith mistakes being read as violations.

brainstormer_pool.label: Annual schedule-setting session with mid-year check-in
brainstormer_pool.rationale: Proactive rather than reactive approach. Parents sit down together (or with mediator) to map major events well in advance. Mid-year check-in allows adjustments. Reduces frequency of notice conflicts by front-loading planning.

party_interest_alignment.party_a: Gets predictability and advance planning for major disruption points. Reduces ongoing anxiety about surprise changes.
party_interest_alignment.party_b: Consolidates planning burden into scheduled sessions rather than ongoing negotiation. May prefer defined planning windows to continuous back-and-forth.

brainstormer_pool.label: Notice requirement with automatic approval for Category A changes
brainstormer_pool.rationale: Categorizes types of schedule changes. Some categories get automatic approval if notice is met; others require mutual agreement even with notice. Separates procedural compliance from substantive disagreement.

party_interest_alignment.party_a: Notice requirement is enforced but also provides some changes they can count on once notice is given. Reduces uncertainty beyond just timing.
party_interest_alignment.party_b: Clear framework for what they can plan unilaterally versus what requires negotiation. Reduces scope for ongoing conflict over each request.

brainstormer_pool.label: First-refusal protocol for unexpected parent schedule changes
brainstormer_pool.rationale: When one parent's plans change and their scheduled time is affected, other parent gets first option to take that time before alternative care is arranged. Pairs notice with opportunity rather than just constraint.

party_interest_alignment.party_a: Gets advance notice of schedule disruptions and control over how disruption is handled. May increase total time with child.
party_interest_alignment.party_b: Notice requirement comes with benefit — if they need to change plans, other parent may welcome the extra time rather than resisting the change.

brainstormer_pool.label: Child age-gated notice requirements with sunset review
brainstormer_pool.rationale: Notice windows appropriate for child's current developmental stage, with agreement to revisit as child ages. Younger child may need more advance preparation; older child may benefit from more flexibility. Builds in evolution rather than permanence.

party_interest_alignment.party_a: Addresses their child-focused framing by tying notice to child's actual needs. Gets predictability now with recognition that needs may change.
party_interest_alignment.party_b: Avoids feeling locked into arrangement that may become unnecessarily rigid. Acknowledges that what works for a 5-year-old differs from a teenager.

safety_monitor_result.monitor_notes: Insufficient interaction history for pattern detection. Only one substantive party turn (T2) exists. Parent A has made an independent statement about their interest (notice window for holiday/school-break changes), but there is no second party statement yet to establish interaction dynamics. No compliance pattern can be assessed without observing how parties respond to each other. No deflection pattern can exist without a disclosure request and response sequence. No discordant signal sequence can occur without multiple back-and-forth exchanges. Monitoring will continue as interaction history develops.

party_a.emotional_state: task-focused and contained, possibly underlying anxiety about predictability
inferred_interests: Predictability and routine for the child
Control over scheduling to avoid disruption
Clear boundaries around timing and notice
inferred_concerns: The other parent may make spontaneous changes that destabilize the child
Lack of advance notice creating anxiety for child or themselves
Loss of ability to plan around custody arrangements
unsaid_signals: Led with a specific procedural concern rather than broader relational or emotional content — may be testing the waters or protecting themselves by starting with logistics
Framed concern around child's experience ('so the child is not surprised') rather than their own — may be either genuinely child-focused or using child welfare as safer framing for their own anxiety
party_a.relational_posture: engaged and assertive on specific issue

unsaid_signals: Has not responded to Party A's concern yet — their reaction to the notice-window issue will be highly diagnostic
Complete absence of signal at this stage

perception_agent_result.relational_dynamic: too early to assess — only one party has spoken substantively; Party A has set an agenda item but the relational field depends on how Party B responds
perception_signals: Party A opened with a concrete procedural concern rather than emotional content or broader relationship issues
The specificity of Party A's concern (notice windows, last-minute shifts) suggests this may be a live issue or recent source of conflict
Party B's response at T3 will be critical for understanding whether this is a shared concern, a point of contention, or something Party B has different priorities around
perception_agent_result.scaffold_divergence: Scaffold assessed Party B as 'engaged_and_cooperative' but Party B has not yet contributed substantively — engagement quality cannot be assessed from silence. Scaffold may be inferring from mere presence, but actual engagement requires observable participation.
perception_notes: Party B has not yet spoken — T3 must create space for Party B to respond to Party A's concern and/or introduce their own priorities
Party A's concern is specific and procedural — before moving to solutions, mediator should explore whether this reflects a pattern of past conflict or is a proactive request
Watch for whether Party B validates Party A's concern, contests it, or shifts to a different topic — this will reveal the underlying relational dynamic
Very limited signal at this turn — confidence should be low; avoid over-interpreting from a single data point

turns.message_summary: Parent B says flexibility matters too, but that written confirmation and preserved makeup time could make changes feel fairer.

positions_structured.statement: Any school-break changes should preserve meaningful time and avoid one-sided loss of time.

turns.message_summary: Acknowledge the structural tension both parties have identified (predictability vs. flexibility), then introduce three tentative structural options for them to react to — tiered notice, makeup time standard, written confirmation — with each option explicitly connected to the specific party interest it serves. Frame this as moving to 'ideas to react to' rather than solutions to accept.

turns.confidence_note: High confidence in option readiness and party state assessment. Moderate confidence in option selection — the three options presented are structurally distinct and well-grounded in party interests, but the child notification standard may emerge as important when parties react. The response framing ('ideas to react to') correctly preserves party ownership and invites refinement rather than acceptance.
turns.message_text:
Thank you both — you've both put something important on the table, and I want to name the tension I'm hearing before we go further. [Party A], you're focused on predictability and advance notice so your child has time to prepare for changes. [Party B], you're focused on preserving flexibility while making changes feel fair through mechanisms like written confirmation and makeup time. Those aren't opposing goals, but they do create a structural question: how do we build a system that gives [Party A] the stability you need while giving [Party B] the adaptability you need?

You've both already started suggesting pieces of a structure — notice windows, written confirmation, makeup time. I want to put a few specific ideas on the table for you to react to. These aren't proposals I'm recommending — they're frameworks to help us test whether we're on the right track.

First: a tiered notice system. Short notice or same-day for emergencies — things like a child's illness or a true crisis. Longer advance notice — say, a week or two — for discretionary changes like work travel or family events. That would address your need for predictability, [Party A], while preserving [Party B]'s ability to respond when something urgent comes up.

Second: a makeup time standard. If one of you requests a change that the other agrees to, the displaced time gets made up within a defined window — maybe the next month, maybe calculated as a rolling credit. [Party B], that's the fairness mechanism you mentioned. [Party A], it also means changes don't erode your total parenting time, which supports stability.

Third: written confirmation for any agreed change — could be text, email, or a shared app — with key details like the date, the reason, and the makeup plan if applicable. [Party B], you mentioned this directly. [Party A], it also creates clarity so your child isn't hearing different things from each of you.

I want to pause here and hear what you think. Do any of these ideas feel like they're moving in a useful direction? Are there parts that don't fit what you actually need?

Party A prioritizes advance notice and child preparation; Party B prioritizes adaptability with fairness safeguards. This is a structural tension, not a relational conflict. The dynamic is healthy — they are problem-solving a real coordination challenge.

domain_analysis.domain_notes: The domain reasoner's assessment is correct: this is a ready case for option work. Both parties have articulated the core tension and both have suggested structural mechanisms. The gaps listed are real but do not block option work — they parameterize solutions rather than determine feasibility. The qualified options pool is strong. However, the perception agent's concern about premature convergence is also valid. The parties have jumped to mechanisms without exploring drivers. The mediator faces a process choice: (1) move to option exploration now, using options as a generative tool to surface deeper interests through reaction, or (2) defer options and elicit experiential context first. Given that both parties are solution-focused and non-distressed, and that options can be used as probes rather than settlements, moving to tentative option exploration is appropriate — but it must be framed as 'ideas to react to' rather than 'solutions to choose.'

Written confirmation requirement with format options
Change request protocol with response window
First-right-of-refusal for displaced parenting time (not in qualified pool but addresses Party B's fairness concern)
qualified_options: Tiered notice system — addresses Party A's predictability need while preserving Party B's ability to respond to urgent situations; the tier structure (emergency vs. discretionary) directly maps to the tension both parties identified
Makeup time standard — addresses Party B's explicit concern about 'preserved makeup time' and Party A's implicit concern that changes not become unilateral forfeitures; fairness mechanism for both
Written confirmation requirement — addresses Party B's explicit mention and creates the documentation trail that reduces later disputes (benefits both parties' need for clarity)

response_synthesis.message_text:
Thank you both — you've both put something important on the table, and I want to name the tension I'm hearing before we go further. [Party A], you're focused on predictability and advance notice so your child has time to prepare for changes. [Party B], you're focused on preserving flexibility while making changes feel fair through mechanisms like written confirmation and makeup time. Those aren't opposing goals, but they do create a structural question: how do we build a system that gives [Party A] the stability you need while giving [Party B] the adaptability you need?

You've both already started suggesting pieces of a structure — notice windows, written confirmation, makeup time. I want to put a few specific ideas on the table for you to react to. These aren't proposals I'm recommending — they're frameworks to help us test whether we're on the right track.

First: a tiered notice system. Short notice or same-day for emergencies — things like a child's illness or a true crisis. Longer advance notice — say, a week or two — for discretionary changes like work travel or family events. That would address your need for predictability, [Party A], while preserving [Party B]'s ability to respond when something urgent comes up.

Second: a makeup time standard. If one of you requests a change that the other agrees to, the displaced time gets made up within a defined window — maybe the next month, maybe calculated as a rolling credit. [Party B], that's the fairness mechanism you mentioned. [Party A], it also means changes don't erode your total parenting time, which supports stability.

Third: written confirmation for any agreed change — could be text, email, or a shared app — with key details like the date, the reason, and the makeup plan if applicable. [Party B], you mentioned this directly. [Party A], it also creates clarity so your child isn't hearing different things from each of you.

I want to pause here and hear what you think. Do any of these ideas feel like they're moving in a useful direction? Are there parts that don't fit what you actually need?
options_introduced: Tiered notice system for schedule changes
Makeup time standard with calculation method
Written confirmation requirement with format options
response_synthesis.confidence_note: High confidence in option readiness and party state assessment. Moderate confidence in option selection — the three options presented are structurally distinct and well-grounded in party interests, but the child notification standard may emerge as important when parties react. The response framing ('ideas to react to') correctly preserves party ownership and invites refinement rather than acceptance.

pre_computed_domain_analysis.readiness_rationale: Both parties have articulated process-level concerns about holiday/break schedule modifications. Party A's interests center on predictability, advance notice, and child preparation time. Party B's interests center on flexibility, fairness mechanisms (written confirmation, makeup time), and avoiding rigidity. This is a communication_protocol/schedule_modification issue where both parties have identified the structural tension: predictability vs. adaptability. The issue family (parenting schedule changes) and stated concerns provide sufficient basis to qualify standard structural options. Specific parameters (notice windows, documentation methods, makeup time calculation) will be negotiated by parties, but the structural options are domain-feasible now. No safety veto conditions apply: both parties have contributed substantive interests; no compliance-only pattern exists; no information asymmetry leverage is present; no active safety signals in party state.

qualified_candidates.option_label: Tiered notice system for schedule changes
qualified_candidates.option_description: Different advance notice requirements based on change magnitude or timing — e.g., routine adjustments vs. major holiday swaps; shorter notice for emergency situations with defined makeup provisions
qualified_candidates.feasibility_rationale: Both parties have identified the predictability-flexibility tension. Party A wants advance notice for child preparation; Party B wants flexibility for life circumstances. Tiered systems are standard in parenting plans for this exact issue type. Structure addresses both concerns: predictability through notice requirements, flexibility through tiered thresholds. Specific tiers (72h/1week/2weeks, emergency definitions, change categories) are party decisions.
conditions: Parties to define specific notice windows for each tier
Parties to define what constitutes 'emergency' or 'routine' change
Parties to agree on whether tier is determined by timing (proximity to holiday) or magnitude (duration of change)

qualified_candidates.option_label: Makeup time standard with calculation method
qualified_candidates.option_description: When one parent's time is reduced due to a change, the other parent's time is adjusted to preserve the overall balance — with defined calculation method (hour-for-hour, day-for-day, or within-period flexibility)
qualified_candidates.feasibility_rationale: Party B explicitly mentioned 'preserved makeup time' as a fairness mechanism. Party A's concern is child stability, not zero-sum time protection, so makeup provisions may address Party A's fairness concern while giving Party B the flexibility sought. Standard mechanism in parenting plans. Specific calculation method (immediate makeup vs. end-of-month reconciliation, strict hour-matching vs. approximate balancing) is a party decision.
conditions: Parties to define calculation method (strict vs. approximate)
Parties to define timing window for makeup (immediate, same month, same quarter)
Parties to agree whether makeup is mandatory or optional depending on circumstances

qualified_candidates.option_label: Written confirmation requirement with format options
qualified_candidates.option_description: Schedule changes must be documented in writing (text, email, shared calendar, co-parenting app) before taking effect, creating a clear record and confirmation step
qualified_candidates.feasibility_rationale: Party B explicitly mentioned 'written confirmation' as a fairness/clarity mechanism. Party A's concern about last-minute changes suggests documentation could provide the predictability sought (changes become 'real' only when confirmed in writing, preventing ambiguity). Standard practice in high-structure parenting plans. Specific format (text vs. app vs. email) and whether verbal agreements can be ratified afterward are party decisions.
conditions: Parties to define acceptable documentation formats
Parties to agree on timing of confirmation (before or after verbal discussion)
Parties to define what happens if confirmation is not provided (change is void, presumed accepted, or case-by-case)

qualified_candidates.option_label: Change request protocol with response window
qualified_candidates.option_description: Party requesting a change must provide specific information (proposed dates, reason, makeup offer if applicable) and other party has defined window to respond; silence after window may be treated as acceptance, rejection, or trigger for follow-up depending on agreement
qualified_candidates.feasibility_rationale: Party A's focus on 'notice window' suggests a structured request process would provide the predictability sought. Party B's flexibility concern suggests they need a way to make requests without feeling they'll be automatically rejected. A request protocol creates a predictable process without predetermining outcomes. Standard mechanism in communication protocols. Specific information requirements, response window duration, and default rule for non-response are party decisions.
conditions: Parties to define what information must be included in change request
Parties to define response window duration
Parties to agree on default rule if no response is received
Parties to define whether certain types of changes have different protocols (emergency vs. routine)

qualified_candidates.option_description: Once a schedule change is agreed, parents follow a defined approach to informing the child — who tells them, when, and what language is used to frame the change
qualified_candidates.feasibility_rationale: Party A's specific concern about 'the child being surprised by last-minute shifts' suggests the child's experience of changes (not just the parental negotiation) is a priority. A child notification standard addresses this directly. Common in parenting plans where child's psychological experience is explicitly prioritized. Specific approach (both parents together, requesting parent alone, age-appropriate timing) is a party decision and may depend on child's age/temperament.
conditions: Parties to define who informs the child (requesting parent, both parents, or case-by-case)
Parties to define timing relative to the change (immediately after agreement, X days before change, depends on child's age)
Parties to agree on framing language or approach (neutral facts, reasons explained, child's input sought)

material_gaps.gap: Current parenting schedule structure (alternating weeks, 2-2-3, specific holiday allocation)
material_gaps.what_it_blocks: Calibrating notice windows and makeup time provisions to the existing schedule cadence. For example, a 2-2-3 schedule may need shorter notice windows than alternating weeks because changes affect near-term days. However, this gap does not block structural option qualification — it affects parameter calibration, which parties will negotiate.

material_gaps.gap: Past experiences with schedule changes (what triggered Party A's concern, what flexibility situations Party B anticipates)
material_gaps.what_it_blocks: Understanding whether the issue is about specific recurring situations (work travel, extended family events, emergencies) or general process dissatisfaction. This would help tailor option parameters (e.g., different rules for different change types) but does not block structural qualification — the process concern is clear regardless of past specifics.

Calibrating child notification standards and assessing how much advance notice is psychologically meaningful for the child. However, this is a parameter refinement issue, not a structural feasibility blocker. Options are qualifiable now; age-appropriate implementation details come during negotiation.

pre_computed_domain_analysis.domain_notes: This is a classic schedule modification process issue in parenting mediation. Both parties have articulated the structural tension (predictability vs. flexibility) that these issues always present. The qualified options are standard mechanisms from family mediation practice for this issue type. Confidence is high because: (1) the issue family is clearly identified, (2) both parties have contributed process-level interests, (3) the structural options address the stated concerns regardless of specific parameters, and (4) no domain constraints (legal, financial, logistical) prevent any of these options. The material gaps are parameter-calibration gaps, not structural feasibility gaps. Parties are ready to see structural options and negotiate parameters. This is exactly the situation where option work is appropriate — clear issue type, bilateral participation, standard option space, parameter negotiation deferred to parties as it should be.

safety_monitor_result.monitor_notes: Insufficient turn history for pattern detection (only 2 substantive client turns). Both parties have made single statements expressing independent interests. Party A initiated with a concern about notice windows and predictability for the child. Party B responded with their own substantive content: introduced the value of flexibility while proposing specific mechanisms (written confirmation, makeup time) to address fairness concerns. Party B's response shows independent thinking - they didn't simply accept or modify Party A's framing, but introduced a balancing concern (flexibility vs. predictability) along with concrete proposals. No compliance pattern detected. No information asymmetry or disclosure requests have occurred that would trigger deflection analysis. No discordant signals have been raised and suppressed. This appears to be normal early-stage bilateral participation in mediation. Continued monitoring recommended as session progresses.

The dynamic is polite and solution-focused, possibly moving toward negotiation before both parties' underlying needs are fully explored.

perception_agent_result.scaffold_divergence: Scaffold assessment of 'cooperative_and_stable' is accurate at surface level, but misses the emerging values tension between predictability and flexibility. Both parties ARE cooperative, but they have introduced competing priorities that have not yet been reconciled or even fully articulated. The 'moderate stress' assessment may be accurate physiologically but doesn't capture that both parties are emotionally contained and procedurally focused — genuine stress signals are not yet visible in the communication patterns.
perception_notes: Party A has set the agenda with a structure-focused concern. Party B has responded constructively BUT with a competing value. Before generating options, the mediator should surface this tension explicitly and explore what drives each party's position.

Party B's flexibility interest is stated abstractly. The mediator should create space for Party B to articulate what kind of flexibility they need and why it matters — otherwise solutions may not address the actual need.

Both parties are solution-focused at T4. The risk is premature convergence on mechanisms (notice windows, written confirmation, makeup time) before the underlying interests and concerns are fully explored.
Neither party has shared past experiences or emotional context. The interests articulated so far are rational/procedural. Depth is available but has not been invited.

turns.message_summary: Both parents are conditionally open to an advance-notice and written-confirmation package for school-break scheduling.

positions_structured.statement: Both parents are open to an advance-notice and written-confirmation package for school-break scheduling.

final_state_summary.continuation_recommendation: Use the next session to continue clarifying positions, interests, and process expectations without overstating agreement.
